macro_segments.title: Three Stages of Ona'ah and the Time Limit Principle (Ad She-Yeira)

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Three Stages: Under a Sixth (Mechila), Exactly a Sixth (Ona'ah), and More Than a Sixth (Bitul Mekach)
The Principle of Ad She-Yeira (Until One Can Show to Friends/Relatives) and Its Application to Ona'ah
The Question: Does Ad She-Yeira Apply Automatically to Mechila and Bitul Mekach?

macro_segments.title: The Rabbi Tarfon Story: How a Change in the Definition of Ona'ah Affects Merchant Preference

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Tarfon's Position: Expanding Ona'ah to One-Third and Its Initial Appeal to Merchants
Why Merchants Initially Preferred Rabbi Tarfon's One-Third: Avoiding Perpetual Bitul Mekach (Le'olam Chozer)
Rabbi Tarfon's Second Ruling: Granting a Whole Day (Kol Ha-Yom) and Why Merchants Reversed Their Position
Distinguishing Between Two Possible Readings: Does Mechila Happen Automatically or Only After Ad She-Yeira?
The Gemara's Resolution: Mechila is Automatic, but the Definition of Ona'ah Changes Between Rabbi Tarfon and the Rabbanan

macro_segments.title: The Unresolved Debates: Is Mechila Automatic? Is Bitul Mekach Le'olam or Limited by Ad She-Yeira?

micro_segments.title: The Two Outstanding Questions: Mechila's Nature and Bitul Mekach's Duration
Why Merchants Preferred Rabanan Over Rabbi Tarfon If Both Have Limited Time Periods
The Gemara's Resolution: Bitul Mekach is Not Common; the Difference Between Kol Ha-Yom and Le'olam is Negligible

macro_segments.title: Rava's Ruling: The Practical Halacha on Mechila, Ona'ah, and Bitul Mekach

micro_segments.title: Rava's Statement: Under One-Sixth is Mechila (Automatic); Exactly One-Sixth is Ona'ah; More Than One-Sixth is Bitul Mekach
The Difference Between Ona'ah and Bitul Mekach: Options Available and Whether Both Sides Can Initiate Retraction
Debate Between Rabbi Natan and Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi: Can the Cheated Party Opt for Bitul Mekach or Only Receive Their Overpayment?
Rashi vs. Tosafos: Does Ona'ah Include the Option of Bitul Mekach, and Is Bitul Mekach Limited by Ad She-Yeira?

macro_segments.title: The Asymmetry Between Buyer and Seller: Why Each Has Different Time Periods to Reverse

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's Rule: The Buyer Has Only Ad She-Yeira (Limited Time), While the Seller Can Always Reverse (Le'olam Chozer)
The Story of Rami Bar Chama's Innkeeper: Illustrating the Seller's Difficulty in Discovering Undercharging
The Reason for Asymmetry: The Buyer Can Display Goods to Others and Discover Cheating Quickly; The Seller Cannot Easily Assess Value

macro_segments.title: The Irony of Ona'ah and Bitul Mekach: Gaming the System by Intentionally Overpaying

micro_segments.title: The Brazen Buyer's Strategy: Intentionally Overpay to Trigger Ona'ah and Get the Full Overage Back Rather Than Accept Mechila
Rav Chisda's Response: This Strategy Only Works When Buying from a Merchant, Not from a Homeowner Selling Personal Effects

macro_segments.title: The Homeowner's Exemption: Personal Goods Have No Ona'ah

micro_segments.title: The Rule: When a Homeowner Sells Personal Items (Not Commodities), Ona'ah Does Not Apply
The Distinction: Canvas Bags and Commodities Still Have Ona'ah; Only Treasured Personal Items Are Exempt
The Logic: Personal Items Are Dear to the Owner and Are Expected to Command a Premium Beyond Market Price

macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Merchant and Homeowner in Ona'ah Laws

micro_segments.title: Ona'ah Rules Apply Equally to All Buyers and Sellers: Whether Merchant or Homeowner (Mishna and Gemara Interpretation)

macro_segments.title: The Biblical Source and Reasoning for Ona'ah Restrictions

micro_segments.title: Deriving Ona'ah from Vayikra 25:14: 'When You Sell or Buy, Do Not Cheat' Applies to Both Buyer and Seller
Why Both Sides Are Restricted: The Seller Knows the Business; The Buyer Is Unsophisticated and Money Is Ephemeral
The Gemara's Economic Philosophy: Objects Held Are More Permanent Than Cash, Which 'Burns a Hole in Your Pocket'

macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Merchant and Homeowner in Ona'ah Laws (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda's Exception: A Professional Merchant (Tagar) Has No Ona'ah Rights Because They Know Their Business
The Definition of 'Merchant' for This Exemption: A Broker or Middleman Who Buys and Resells Immediately (Safsar)
The Logic Behind the Merchant Exemption: A Professional Merchant Cannot Claim Ignorance and Must Know Their Pricing

macro_segments.title: Alternative Interpretation by Rav Ashi: The Merchant's Advantage (Opposite of Rabbi Yehuda's View)

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Reading: A Merchant Actually Has More Rights, Not Fewer—Even a Small Undercharge Entitles Them to Recover
The Reason: Because It Is the Merchant's Livelihood (Parnassah), They Are Entitled to Full Value Protection

macro_segments.title: Attributing the Mishna and Baraita to Different Authorities

micro_segments.title: The Question: Is Our Mishna According to Rabbi Natan or Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi, Given the Apparent Contradiction with the Baraita?
Rava's Conclusion: The Mishna is Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi; Both Cases (Buyer Cheated, Seller Cheated) Are Implied in the Mishna's Opening Statement
Rav Ashi's Proof: The Mishna's Opening 'Echad Ha-Lokeach Ve-Echad Ha-Mocher' Implies Both Examples Are Equally Valid

topical_tags.term: Ad She-Yeira (Until One Can Show to Friends/Relatives)
